coredata.dc:title: Low mass loading of graphene quantum dots on titanium nitride nanotube arrays for boosting capacity and operating voltage of symmetric supercapacitor in an aqueous electrolyte
coredata.dc:creator: Mouchani, Parastoo
Sarraf-Mamoory, Rasoul
Aghajani, Hossein
Ahadzadeh, Iraj
coredata.dc:description: Compared to other supercapacitors, aqueous supercapacitors offer reliability, a long lifecycle, and affordability. However, the significant challenge in advancing aqueous supercapacitors lies in their limited operating voltage window. This limitation has resulted in a notable reduction in energy density. Therefore, contemporary electrode materials exhibiting high electrochemical activity and wide operating potential windows are imperative. This study introduces a technique that not only boosts energy density but also signifies a significant breakthrough for metal nitride-based aqueous supercapacitors. This method broadens the operating voltage range and necessitates optimization of the heterostructure design. A distinctive titanium nitride/graphene quantum dot (TiN/GQD) heterostructure was synthesized through electrophoretic deposition of GQDs onto plasma-nitrided TiO2 nanotubes. This novel heterostructure demonstrates the capability to function across an extended voltage range of 2 V. It is noteworthy that the TiN/GQD nanocomposite has excellent bonding at the interface of TiOC and a large surface area, resulting in an exceptional areal capacitance of 190 mF cm−2 at a current density of 0.2 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. It also displays excellent cycle stability (91.5 % after 6000 galvanostatic charge-discharge cycles). Moreover, the optimum GQDs deposition time (15 min) was determined to achieve the most efficient electrochemical performance. Furthermore, a symmetric TiN/GQD-15 supercapacitor device was assembled, reaching a high energy density of 8.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 77 W kg−1.

head.ce:title: Low mass loading of graphene quantum dots on titanium nitride nanotube arrays for boosting capacity and operating voltage of symmetric supercapacitor in an aqueous electrolyte

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compared to other supercapacitors, aqueous supercapacitors offer reliability, a long lifecycle, and affordability. However, the significant challenge in advancing aqueous supercapacitors lies in their limited operating voltage window. This limitation has resulted in a notable reduction in energy density. Therefore, contemporary electrode materials exhibiting high electrochemical activity and wide operating potential windows are imperative. This study introduces a technique that not only boosts energy density but also signifies a significant breakthrough for metal nitride-based aqueous supercapacitors. This method broadens the operating voltage range and necessitates optimization of the heterostructure design. A distinctive titanium nitride/graphene quantum dot (TiN/GQD) heterostructure was synthesized through electrophoretic deposition of GQDs onto plasma-nitrided TiO2 nanotubes. This novel heterostructure demonstrates the capability to function across an extended voltage range of 2 V. It is noteworthy that the TiN/GQD nanocomposite has excellent bonding at the interface of TiOC and a large surface area, resulting in an exceptional areal capacitance of 190 mF cm−2 at a current density of 0.2 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. It also displays excellent cycle stability (91.5 % after 6000 galvanostatic charge-discharge cycles). Moreover, the optimum GQDs deposition time (15 min) was determined to achieve the most efficient electrochemical performance. Furthermore, a symmetric TiN/GQD-15 supercapacitor device was assembled, reaching a high energy density of 8.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 77 W kg−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Past research has focused on developing of renewable and sustainable energy technologies, ranging from fuel cells to batteries and supercapacitors [1,2]. Supercapacitors are alternatives to batteries and fuel cells owing to their high charge/discharge (CD) rate, high power density, extended life cycle, and favorable reversibility [3]. Supercapacitors can be classified into two groups based on how they store energy: electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors. However, EDLCs have a disadvantage because they have a low energy density [4], whereas pseudocapacitors suffer from low power density and poor cycling stability [5,6]. To address the constraints posed by the prior two iterations of supercapacitors, the suggested strategy involves employing hybrid supercapacitors, which fall under the category of the third generation [7]. Hybrid electrodes were formed by combining two or three components of nanostructured conductive polymers, metal oxides, and carbon-based materials [8–10]. The considerable focus on incorporating transition metal nitrides into supercapacitors arises from their remarkable conductivity and distinctive chemical bonding, which collectively lead to their enhanced physicochemical characteristics [11]. Titanium nitride (TiN) and titanium oxynitride (TiOxN1-x) are two transition metal nitrides that have been extensively studied because of their impressive electrical conductivity, wide voltage window, and chemical resistance to a broad pH range [12–14]. Although it is difficult to achieve high energy density and high power density simultaneously with these materials, one of the most efficient and common approaches to achieve high-performance TiN-based electrodes with faster charge/discharge rates, enhanced power density, and high rate capabilities is the use of carbonaceous materials with a double-layer charge storage mechanism [15].

Carbon nanomaterials provide large surface areas, excellent mechanical stability, and rapid current response to potential changes. They store charge only on their surface, and thus exhibit high power density and cyclability [16–19]. Among carbon derivatives such as carbon nanotubes, activated carbon, graphene, and carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are more attractive owing to their unique features. The significant advantages of GQDs are their excellent dispersibility in both aqueous and non-aqueous solvents, low toxicity, ultra-small size with high surface area, edge effect, and more active sites, which can further boost supercapacitive properties of pseudocapacitive electrode materials [20–23]. Due to the energy edge effects, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit the capacity for possessing a greater variety of covalent and non-covalent functional groups [24,25]. Meanwhile, they can absorb more charge at the edge than at the basal plane [26]; therefore, GQDs are crucial for charge transfer processes within electrode materials.

Heterostructured electrodes based on GQDs and pseudocapacitive materials demonstrate promising capacitance properties [27–31]. For example, the core/shell of Co3O4/graphene quantum dots has a specific capacitance of 2435 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, and their capacity increases by up to 112 % over 4500 cycles [32]. Moreover, GQD-doped CuCo2S4 nanocomposites demonstrated a specific capacitance of 1725 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 and very high cycle stability (90% retention) after 10,000 cycles by changing the morphology of the CuCo2S4 nanosheets [33]. It has been demonstrated that nanostructures of NiCo2S4/GQD lead to enhanced electrochemical performance. This improvement encompasses a specific capacitance of 678.22 F g−1 at a current density of 0.2 A g−1, along with maintaining 94 % cycle stability after 5000 cycles. This phenomenon can be clarified by the presence of additional interspaces and pathways facilitating ion diffusion, coupled with volume fluctuations, reduced impedance, and a larger specific surface area [34]. The NiCo2O4/GQDs nanocomposite had a measured specific capacitance of 481.4 F g−1 at 0.35 A g−1, attributed to the increased electrical conductivity and quicker ion transport between the electrode surfaces and the electrolyte [35].

To the best of our knowledge, although titanium nitride-based supercapacitor electrodes have been extensively studied in the literature [36–41], no study has been conducted on the influence of adding GQDs to TiN to produce nanocomposite materials with enhanced supercapacitivity.

In this research, a unique electrode material was employed to fabricate a symmetric supercapacitor, comprising an arrangement of titanium nitride nanotubes and graphene quantum dots. This is the first time such a combination has been employed for this purpose. In this process, GQDs were uniformly loaded onto TiN nanotubes by the electrophoretic deposition (EPD) method without any additives. Adding GQDs to TiN nanotubes has led to the development of energy storage devices with high operating voltages in aqueous electrolytes. The electrochemical performance was assessed by comparing the nanocomposite electrode to the unmodified TiN electrode.

GQDs were produced following previously reported procedures with minor modifications [42]. In summary, 2 g of citric acid were placed in a 5 ml beaker and subjected to heating in an electric oven at 200 °C for 30 min, resulting in its transformation to an orange color. In order to achieve a pH level of 9, the resulting liquid was slowly introduced into 100 ml of a 10 mg ml−1 NaOH solution under continuous stirring. Successfully synthesized GQDs can be stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for a month without any changes; however, in another step, the suspension solvent was removed with a freeze dryer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiN nanotubes and TiN/GQD nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: According to our previous work [43], TiN nanotubes were synthesized by anodizing a Ti sheet and the plasma nitriding of TiO2 nanotubes. Briefly, mirror-finished Ti sheets of 1 × 2 cm2 were degreased and then treated with a solution of HNO3, H2O, and HF (50:40:10 vol%) for 6 s to eliminate the natural oxides. For the anodization step, two parallel Ti and graphite sheets were immersed in ethylene glycol and a 0.3 wt% ammonium fluoride electrolyte for 3 h at a constant voltage of 60 V. After completing the three anodizing steps, the samples were calcined at 450 °C for 2 h. Plasma nitriding was performed under an N2-H2 atmosphere for 5 h at 650 °C.

The TiN/GQD nanocomposite was prepared using a straightforward electrophoretic deposition process in a standard two-electrode cell [44]. To prepare the electrophoretic electrolyte, 1 mg of graphene quantum dots were dispersed in 8 ml of DI water, to which a 0.5 M NaOH solution was added. A 30-minute sonication process was used to disperse the resulting mixture completely. A parallel Ti sheet with dimensions of 3 × 2 cm2 and a distance of 2 cm was used as a counter electrode, and a +4 V potential was applied to the TiN working electrode to deposit the GQDs onto the TiN substrate. Various deposition times of 15, 30, and 60 min were employed to generate three distinct samples: TiN/GQD-15, TiN/GQD-30, and TiN/GQD-60, respectively. The samples were then dried at room temperature after being washed with ethanol. The weight of the active material for TiN, TiN/GQD-15, TiN/GQD-30, and TiN/GQD-60 is 1.11, 1.31, 1.44 and 1.51 mg cm−2, respectively. These values were obtained by anodizing a large Ti sheet and dividing the weight by the sheet area.

The morphologies and nanostructures of the synthesized nanoparticles and nanocomposites were analyzed using various methods. These techniques encompass field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), performed using a Tescan Mira3 at 30 kV, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), carried out with a PHILIPS CM120 at 100 kV. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were generated using an XRD diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.1542 nm) as the X-ray source (Bruker), with a diffraction angle range of 10–80°. Raman spectra were obtained by recording backscattered light from a 532 nm argon-ion laser using a TakRam N1-541 spectrometer (Teksan). Finally, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a Specs model EA10 instrument (BESTEC). The binding energy changes were compensated for by surface-charging effects in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using the C1s level (284.6 eV) as an internal reference. An Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) Nanosurf Mobile S device, manufactured by Nanosurf Company, was employed to assess the dimensions and thickness of the prepared GQDs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired using a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrometric analyzer (Thermo) within the range of 400–4000 cm−1, utilizing KBr pellets. The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the GQD were ascertained through an Analytik Jena Specord 250 Plus UV–Vis spectrophotometer, employing a scan range spanning 190–1000 nm. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was executed using a Q600 SDT-TA Instruments apparatus, with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1, encompassing the temperature range of 30–800 °C, and conducted under an air atmosphere.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge analysis (GCDs), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed using an Autolab PGSTAT302N potentiostat/galvanostat to examine the electrochemical characteristics of the prepared devices. The electrolyte solution used was 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. The experimental setup involved a three-electrode system, with a Pt electrode as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The prepared samples, with an active surface area of 1 × 1 cm2, were applied directly as the working electrode.

A symmetrical supercapacitor device with dimensions of 1 × 1 cm2 was constructed by sandwiching two nanocomposite electrodes with a cellulose filter paper separator. To optimize the potential window of the symmetric supercapacitor device, analyses were conducted over various potential ranges. Consequently, the potential window of 0–2 V was selected as the operating voltage for the cell. Furthermore, in order to evaluate the performance of the cell, tests were conducted within both the positive and negative potential windows. No significant changes or deviations were observed in the experimental results, suggesting that the symmetric supercapacitor device developed in this study is of the non-polar type. In addition, by employing CV, Chronopotentiometry, and EIS, the entire cell was identified.

Based on the following equations and the discharge curves, the areal specific capacitance (Ca, mF cm−2) for three-electrode configurations and the gravimetric specific capacitance (Cs,cell, F g −1) for the supercapacitor cell were calculated:

In this case, we reported both areal and gravimetric specific capacitance when describing the device performance, which reduces the risk of inaccurate measurements owing to the low-density materials on the electrode [45,46]. where Ccell is the specific capacitance (F g−1) calculated from the GCD measurements, Δt is the discharge time (s), and ΔV is the potential window (V).

FE-SEM was used to determine the morphology of the nanocomposites. The SEM image in Fig. 1a indicates that TiN has a nanotube morphology with a smooth surface and diameter of ∼120 nm. Fig. S1 shows the TEM image and histogram of the particle size distribution of the synthesized GQDs, which had a mean size of 4.6 nm. The bright spots in the AFM image (Fig. S2) indicate the presence of nanosized GQDs. The AFM image shows that the sample has a maximum height of 1.5 nm, indicating that these particles are either single layer or have a maximum of two to three layers [47]. Furthermore, the GQDs were identified using Raman, FT-IR, UV–Vis, and photoluminescence analyses, and their spectra and relevant information are provided in the Supplementary Information (Figs. S3, S4, and S5). To express the importance of the GQDs loading method, Fig. S6 shows SEM images of the TiN/GQD nanocomposite prepared by immersion of nanotubes in the GQD solution at two different magnifications. As shown in Fig. S6, in the immersion deposition method, the quantum dots were aggregated and agglomerated non-uniformly in various places on the surface of the nanotubes, and some parts were not covered. It is clear that in the immersed sample, the outlet path of the nanotubes is covered in some regions. Therefore, the electrophoretic deposition method was used to create a uniform layer of GQDs. In the presence of an electric field, the negatively charged graphene particles move toward the working electrode and are well and uniformly connected to the charged surface of the nanotubes.

The SEM images in Fig. 1b–d illustrate the TiN/GQD nanocomposites, prepared by electrophoretic deposition of GQDs at various times. These images demonstrate that the GQDs were evenly deposited on the nanotube surfaces. In the EPD method, varying quantities of GQDs are deposited onto the nanotubes based on the duration of the deposition process. After nitriding, the inner diameter of the nanotubes is approximately 100 nm. The morphology of the TiN nanotubes remains unaltered following the deposition of the GQD. However, the average inner diameter of the nanotubes was reduced to approximately 70 nm for TiN/GQD-30 and 55 nm for TiN/GQD-60. No significant reduction in inner diameter was observed for the TiN/GQD-15 sample. To obtain additional information about the morphology of the TiN/GQD nanocomposites, TiN/GQD-15 was investigated using TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 . As can be seen, TiN nanotubes have very smooth walls without nanoparticles on them. The inner diameter of the nanotube and wall thickness were approximately 88 and 34 nm, respectively. Fig. 2b shows the nanotubes after loading with the graphene quantum dots. As evident, the GQDs were completely attached to the inner surface of the nanotubes, forming a GQD layer with an approximate thickness of 16 nm. Notably, the GQD loading did not block the inner path of the nanotubes, making it easier for electrolyte ions to penetrate the entire length of the nanotubes.

Fig. 3a displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the TiN nanotubes and the TiN/GQD nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 3a, the peaks labeled with the heart symbol are positioned between those of TiN (JCPDS 038-1420) and TiO (JCPDS 008-0117), which are attributed to TiN/TiO1-xNx (almost non-stoichiometric TiN). This topic has been extensively analyzed in our previous research [43]. The number and position of the peaks in the XRD pattern of the TiN/GQD nanocomposites remained unchanged, while there was only a slight reduction in the peak intensity because of the presence of GQDs. Raman analysis was performed to confirm the presence of the GQDs.

Fig. 3b shows the Raman spectra of TiN and TiN/GQD nanocomposites. The TiN sample shows three strong peaks in the range of 219, 292, and 575 cm−1, which are related to transverse acoustic (TA), longitudinal acoustic (LA), and transverse optical (TO) phonons [48]. However, the TiN/GQD nanocomposite, in addition to three acoustic and optical peaks, shows two new peaks at 1359 and 1600 cm−1, which correspond to the D and G characteristics of the GQDs [44]. The existence of peaks D and G proves the successful deposition of GQDs on the surface of the nanotubes. The ratio of the D to G peaks remained unchanged after EPD, demonstrating that this technique had no significant effect on the intrinsic structures of GQD and TiN, as shown by a comparison of the Raman spectra of the synthesized GQD (Fig. S3 (a)) with TiN/GQD.

The TGA results for TiN nanotubes and TiN/GQD nanocomposites with different EPD times are shown in Fig. 3c. Experiments were conducted over a temperature range of 30–800 °C to evaluate the thermal stability of the samples and to determine the appropriate quantity of GQDs loaded onto the TiN nanotubes. Fig. 3c shows no weight loss was observed, and TiN remained entirely stable in the temperature range of 100–800 °C. The composite samples containing GQDs exhibited weight loss occurring in two distinct stages. The weight loss observed in the first stage, which was present in all three samples and occurred before 120 °C, could be associated with the evaporation of trapped water molecules. The weight loss observed within the temperature range of 450–650 °C was more significant and was related to the combustion and decomposition of the graphene dots. The performances of the three composites were comparable, and the only difference was in the GQD concentration. Specifically, the composites produced with EPD times of 15, 30, and 60 min exhibited GQD concentrations of approximately 3.4 %, 5 %, and 7.5 %, respectively.

One of the most critical factors for assessing the supercapacitive behavior of various materials is their specific surface areas. The specific surface areas of the TiN nanotubes before and after loading the GQDs were evaluated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method and the N2 gas adsorption-desorption isotherm at 77.36 K (Fig. 4a). According to the IUPAC classification, a typical IV isotherm was observed, suggesting the presence of a mesoporous structure. The calculated specific surface areas of the TiN and TiN/GQD nanocomposite materials were 228 m2 g−1 and 337 m2 g−1, respectively, while the average pore width were 32.2 nm and 18.8 nm. Fig. 4b also shows the pore size distribution plots from the N2 adsorption isotherms of TiN and the Tin/GQD-15 nanocomposites. The TiN/GQD-15 nanocomposite, with its large surface area, provides a more significant number of electroactive sites and facilitates the movement of charges through porous channels, resulting in efficient charge transport. This enhanced surface area is expected to contribute to the nanocomposite's ability to exhibit high energy storage characteristics.

XPS was used to explore the surface elemental composition and chemical states of the TiN nanotubes and the TiN/GQD nanocomposites. Fig. 5a, e illustrate the complete XPS spectra, which demonstrate the existence of Ti, N, O, and C in both TiN and TiN/GQD samples. The C peak in the TiN/GQD nanocomposites exhibited a substantial increase compared to that in the TiN spectra, suggesting that the compositing of GQDs and TiN nanotubes was successful. In Fig. 5b, the Ti 2p spectrum shows TiN peaks at the binding energies of 2p3/2 = 455.6 eV, and 2p1/2 = 461.4 eV, and TiNO peaks at 2p3/2 = 457.2, eV and 2p1/2 = 463.1 eV, and the peaks related to TiO occur at 2p3/2 = 458.8 eV, and 2p1/2 = 465.2 eV. These results show TiO2 nanotubes nitrided under N2 H2 plasma form TiN, TiNO, and TiO. In the O1s spectrum of the TiN nanotubes (Fig. 5c), the prominent peak can be fitted with two peaks associated with TiO and TiNO bonds.

The N 1s spectrum of the TiN nanotubes (Fig. 5d) exhibited a broad peak that could be deconvoluted into two additional peaks. The peaks at the binding energies of 396 and 397 eV correspond to the reported values for TiN and TiNO compounds, respectively. Additionally, in the left section of the N 1s spectrum, an upward shift of the curve was observed at a binding energy of 400 eV, indicating surface adsorption of nitrogen molecules and atoms. A similar trend was observed for the N 1s peaks in the TiN/GQD composite sample (Fig. 5h).

Moreover, Fig. 5f shows that the titanium nitride/oxynitride phases were highly stable during the electrophoretic process. Therefore, there was no change in the location and intensity of the peaks in the Ti 2p spectra after loading the GQDs. The C 1s-XPS spectrum of the nanocomposite shown in Fig. 5i was fitted with four Gaussian peaks centered at 284.5, 286.1, 288.6, and 289.4 eV. The 284.5 eV peak is related to the sp2 hybridization of the honeycomb structure of the carbon atoms. In contrast, the other three peaks were related to oxygenated edge agents [49], as highlighted in the image. Among all the carbon bonds, the ether functional groups COC and COOH are less stable and can rearrange to form bonds during composite formation. Also, the CO peak in the deconvoluted C 1s spectra of TiN/GQD corresponds to hydroxylic groups, which were confirmed by the COH peak at 535.1 V in O 1s spectra. This COH peak is related to the surface bond on the GQDs. Fig. 5j shows the C 1s spectra of the GQDs. The COC (ether) and COOH functional groups were significantly reduced in the nanocomposite sample, implying that some of the oxygen-related functional groups may be converted to in-plane epoxy groups to facilitate the possible formation of COTi bonds in the TiN/GQD nanocomposite. The carbon atoms on the edges were coupled with CO (ether), COOH, and CO oxygen functional groups, with CO being the most stable bond. Because the COC and COOH functional groups are less stable, they may move during nanocomposite formation by applying a potential in the EPD method. Furthermore, the absence of a peak linked to the TiC bond was observed, indicating that Ti and C are bonded through the oxygen atom, potentially creating a TiOC bridge [50], corresponding to the binding energy of 532.1 eV for the present sample (Fig. 5g).

Nanocomposites were investigated electrochemically as electrode components for supercapacitors using CV at varying scan rates, EIS, and GCD measurements at constant currents. A study was conducted to examine the contribution of GQDs to the properties of supercapacitors by analyzing the behavior of TiN nanotubes and TiN/GQD nanocomposites. CV was used to test all the samples at different scan rates ranging from 10 to 200 mV s−1. The results are shown in Fig. 6a, b, and Fig. S7a–c. From these figures, it was observed that the CV curve area of TiN/GQD-15 was much higher than those of the other nanocomposite samples, suggesting that it had the highest specific capacitance. For a more precise comparison, the CV curves of TiN and TiN/GQD-15 are shown in Fig. 6a. The CV curve of TiN/GQD-15 at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 is relatively rectangular and has a larger area as well as an extended potential window of −0.5 to 1.5 V in comparison to pure TiN nanotubes. The CV curve was slightly deformed by increasing the potential window to 1.6 V, the CV curve was somewhat deformed, suggesting that irreversible reactions occurred in this potential zone. Consequently, when GQDs form heterostructures, the potential window for TiN/GQD-15 electrodes can be extended to 2 V. Visual inspection revealed that the counter electrode (Pt foil) produced tiny bubbles between −0.5–1.5 V. However, there was no obvious change in the Pt foil, such as bubble exhaustion, in the electrolytic cell with the TiN/GQD-15 electrode, which suggests that these nanocomposite electrodes exhibit stable performance in this wide potential window.

Next, it can be seen from Fig. 6b that even at high scan rates, the CV curves of the TiN/GQD-15 nanocomposite maintain a rectangular shape, which is proof of fast electron transfer in this porous structure. The decrease in the CV curve areas and lower current density with increasing electrophoretic deposition time of the GQDs followed a downward trend, possibly due to the overloading of quantum dots on the inner wall of the nanotubes and the increase in the dead mass.

The GCD measurements were performed to better understand the performance of the nanocomposite electrodes. GCD results of all prepared electrodes at the differed current densities in the range of 0.2–2 mA cm−2 are represented in Fig. 6c and Fig. S7d–f, which demonstrate the same capacitance trend. In the GCD curve of the TiN/GQD-15 nanocomposite, a deviation from a symmetrical triangular shape was observed, indicating the substantial influence of pseudocapacitance behavior on the electrode surface. In the case of the TiN/GQD-30 electrode, the shape of the charge-discharge curve changed to a nearly perfect triangular shape as the thickness of the GQD layer on the nanotubes increased, demonstrating that the electric double layer was the primary mechanism for charge storage. In fact, with increasing EPD time, the access of electrolyte ions to the surface of TiN nanotubes is restricted to faradaic reactions. Consequently, the discharge time decreased in the GCD curve of TiN/GQD-60 compared to the charge time, possibly because of the agglomeration of GQDs caused by an increase in the deposition time, leading to a reduced specific surface area compared to that of the TiN/GQD-30 nanocomposite. The areal capacitances of all samples, calculated using the GCD curves, are plotted in Fig. 6d. At the current density of 0.2 mA cm−2, the consequent areal capacitance for TiN/GQD-15 was 190 mF cm−2, much higher than TiN nanotubes, TiN/GQD-30, TiN/GQD-60 which have respective areal capacitances of 35, 97.4 and 44.6 mF cm−2.

As the current density increased, the areal capacitance of the electrodes decreased because of improper participation of the active materials in the redox reactions. However, the TiN/GQD-15 nanocomposite retained 89.5 % of its initial capacity even after the current density increased 10 times to 2 mA cm−2. This implies that the electrode, in comparison to the bare TiN electrode, demonstrates exceptional rate capability. This is likely because the electrode has a unique porous structure with a greater specific surface area, which, following the addition of GQDs, provides more active sites and superior electrical conductivity. Rapid current densities during the charge-discharge process are made possible by GQDs, leading to enhanced electron and ion transport and fast electrochemical kinetics. Additionally, the GQDs contributed to sustaining the capacity at high current densities. Notably, even when the pseudocapacitive mechanism is more difficult to achieve, GQDs can still store charge through a double-layer mechanism. Furthermore, in TiN/GQD-30 and TiN/GQD-60, there is an apparent reduction in the rate capability, which could be caused by the separation of graphene dots at high current densities and the difficulties in moving and penetrating the electrolyte ions.

EIS was conducted to gain practical insight into the ion diffusion and conductivity behavior in the frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz. The Nyquist plots displayed a linear trend at low frequencies and an arc shape at high frequencies for all the samples, as shown in Fig. 6e. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the electrode-electrolyte interface was responsible for the semicircles observed. In the high-frequency region, the semicircle diameter of the TiN/GQD-15 sample is significantly smaller than those of the other samples, which indicates a meager charge-transfer resistance against Faradaic reactions. Moreover, along the Z' axis situated on the left side of the plot, the point of intersection with the curve signifies the value of the equivalent series resistance (ESR). The TiN/GQD-15 sample had a much lower ESR value than the TiN electrode and the other two nanocomposite samples. The main reason for the improved transportation of electrons within the system is the modification of GQDs. Moreover, the vertical line observed at low frequencies denotes the diffusive movement of electrolyte ions at the electrode surface. Furthermore, the steeper line observed in the TiN/GQD-15 sample implies less resistance to ion diffusion (accelerated ion diffusion) and closer proximity to the ideal state [51].

The efficiency and lifespan of electrode materials are determined by their cyclic stability during different charge-discharge cycles, which is an important consideration when assessing the performance of supercapacitors. In order to determine the sustained performance of the nanocomposite electrodes across multiple charge-discharge cycles, GCD measurements were carried out, as depicted in Fig. 6f. The electrode consisting of the TiN/GQD-15 nanocomposite maintained 91.5 % of its initial value after undergoing 6000 consecutive cycles, showcasing notably superior cycle stability compared to electrodes composed solely of bare TiN, which retained only 85 % of its initial value. Moreover, the TiN/GQD-15 nanocomposite electrode exhibited exceptional mechanical stability as there was no evidence of peeling off from the Ti substrate. This suggests the hierarchical-like hollow TiN/GQD-15 nanocomposite electrode was mechanically robust. The hollow structure and GQD incorporation both contributed to improved cycling stability. In the TiN electrode, the nanotubes become deformed at their ends owing to the volumetric expansion caused by the entry and exit of ions during the extended cycling. However, in the nanocomposite samples, GQDs inhibited the deformation, dissolution, morphological disorder, and agglomeration of TiN nanotubes during cycling.

To understand the underlying factors behind the electrochemical characteristics of the TiN/GQD nanocomposite electrodes, the impedance data were analyzed using the equivalent circuit provided in the inner part of the Nyquist plot in Figs. 7a and S8a, b. The circuit includes R3, which represents the charge-discharge resistance along the nanotubes, and R1 and CPE1, which are utilized to model the charge transfer resistance (interface between the electrode and electrolyte) and capacitance in the top surface part of the nanotubes. Similarly, R2 and CPE2 were used to simulate the resistance and capacitance data, respectively, in the lower region of the nanotubes. CPE-P is the power of a constant-phase element determined by the properties of the electrode materials. CPE-T indicates the ideal capacitance when CPE-P = 1. The nanotube penetration depth was related to the Warburg impedance (W). Table 1 contains the fitting data of the equivalent circuit diagrams for nanocomposite electrodes made of TiN/GQD-15, TiN/GQD-30, and TiN/GQD-60. According to these findings, an increase in CPE indicates an increase in the electrical conductivity of the sample. As a result, the electrochemical reactions of the electrode occur more quickly, which enhances the performance. Following the CV and GCD results, the CPE value of the electrode increased from TiN/GQD-60 to TiN/GQD-15, exhibiting enhanced efficiency. As shown in Table 1, the values of R3 did not change significantly after adding different amounts of GQDs, possibly at our current level of knowledge, because the structure of the nanotubes remained unchanged during the electrophoretic deposition of GQDs. However, the values for R2 the TiN/GQD-60 nanocomposite were higher than those of the previous samples owing to the increased EPD time. In this case, the bottom of the nanotubes was filled with GQDs, and direct contact between the nanotubes and electrolyte was hindered, leading to charge-transfer resistance. In a similar trend for R1, where TiN/GQD-15 (1.8 Ω.cm2) had lower resistance than TiN/GQD-30 (2.6 Ω.cm2) and TiN/GQD-60 nanocomposites (5.3 Ω.cm2). Because the CPE1-T values for all three samples are very close, it can be concluded that the GQDs act as an electric double layer on the surface of all samples.

A helpful electrochemical analytical technique for investigating the kinetics of electrode reactions is the Dunn method, which can be used to study the voltage response of electroactive materials at various scanning rates through the anodic peaks in the CV curves. By employing this approach, it becomes feasible to distinguish the individual contributions of the energy storage mechanisms associated with the electric double layer and pseudocapacitance. The current density (i(V)) at a constant potential can be determined from the voltametric curve and is expressed as the sum of the two terms in Eq. (5).

. Upon linear fitting, the slope of the obtained line corresponded to k1, whereas k2 was represented by the intercept. As shown in Fig. 7b, the drawn the line at a specified voltage of −0.1 V in relation to the SCE, and the equation of this line are also shown inside. Consequently, the percentages of EDLC and pseudocapacitance contributions of TiN and TiN/GQD-15 can be deduced, as shown in Fig. 7c. As can be seen, by loading GQDs on the surface of nanotubes, the contribution of the double-layer behavior related to graphene particles has increased up to 64 %. This implies that the capacity was boosted due to the combined benefits of both the pseudocapacitance and electric double layer mechanisms. Because of its improved EDLC behavior, the nanocomposite electrode demonstrated more robust response to current at a higher scan rate, and more areal capacitance was accessible.

The TiN/GQD-15 nanocomposite was employed as positive and negative electrodes in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte to develop a symmetric supercapacitor. Fig. 8a shows the CV curve of the device at a scan rate of 10–200 mV s−1 obtained in a voltage window of 0–2 V. The quasi-rectangular shape with a broad redox peak observed at a potential difference of 0.25–0.75 V in the CV curve of the symmetric cell can be attributed to the synergistic interplay between EDLC and pseudocapacitance mechanisms. As shown in Fig. 8b, the GCD curve of the device exhibits pseudocapacitance behavior, which is consistent with the CV curves. The remarkable symmetry displayed by the Galvanostatic Charge-Discharge (GCD) curve further emphasizes the considerable Coulomb efficiency exhibited by this device. Coulomb efficiency, in this context, represents the ratio of discharging time to charging time [52]. The calculated specific capacitances for the symmetric device were 14.7, 13.2, 11.8, 9.5, and 8.1 F g−1 at current densities of 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1, and 2 mA cm−2, respectively. Furthermore, even after 10,000 cycles, the symmetric device exhibited a high capacitance retention rate with only a 10.8 % loss, as shown in Fig. 8c. This is an important consideration when assessing the practical application potential of devices.

Ragone plots of the supercapacitors and other recent studies are shown in Fig. 8d, where the specific power and energy were measured at various current densities. For the purpose of facilitating a more comprehensive comparison, additional findings from other studies were also incorporated. As can be seen, the assembled symmetric supercapacitor delivering the maximum energy density of 8.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 77 W kg−1. Furthermore, when considering a power density of 771 W kg−1, the energy density gradually decreases to 4.5 Wh kg−1. In practical terms, these values are pretty comparable and even higher than those achieved by multi-arc ion plating for TiN arrays on Ni foam (4.72 Wh kg−1, 800 W kg−1) [53], sputtered TiN (5.1 Wh kg−1, 1600 W kg−1), corn-like TiN (0.05 Wh kg−1, 198 W kg−1) [40], TiN in porous Si (1.1 Wh kg−1, 400 W kg−1) [54], TiN on Si nanorod (0.08 Wh kg−1, 39 W kg−1) [55], ZnO/Ti/TiN core-shell nanostructures (0.24 Wh kg−1, 432 W kg−1) [56], polypyrrole grown on V2O5 nanoribbon (20.1 Wh kg−1, 50 W kg−1) [57]. It is worth noting that comparing various supercapacitor devices is not entirely reliable since the cells are assembled in various types and different electrolytes. The capacitance values, energy, and power densities of the supercapacitor are significantly influenced by the type and concentration of the employed electrolytes. As a result, citing such comparisons can be challenging and only provide general insights into supercapacitors with similar active materials from the same class. Therefore, as can be seen from the Ragone plot, devices with TiN electrodes are placed in the supercapacitors category. However, when it comes to TiN-based nanocomposite, this work is placed somewhere in the middle of supercapacitors and Pb-acid batteries, with power density close to that of polypyrrole/V2O5, one of the most significant supercapacitor materials with exceptional features.

The outcomes of this investigation align with the principles of band theory in semiconductor physics. This theory postulates that the introduction of GQDs into the nitride material enhances the conductivity of the composite, particularly facilitating the movement of electrons.

Fig. 9 indicates a schematic energy-band diagram to help understand the relationship between the widening of the potential window and the increase in capacitance caused by the TiN/GQD heterostructure. Because of the quantum confinement effect, the bandgap of GQDs is known to change with particle size. As a result, decreasing the size of the GQDs increases their bandgap. The estimated band gap energy calculated using the photoluminescence spectrum for the GQDs (Fig. S4) is approximately 2.78 eV.

Discrete energy levels can be observed because of the size-dependent bandgap of GQDs. However, it is also likely that the work function of the GQDs is affected by their size. GQDs with a size range of 7–10 nm exhibit a work function of 4.5–4.7 eV, while those with a size range of 3–6 nm have a work function of 5.2 eV [29,58].

When two materials with different work functions come into contact, a built-in electric field is typically formed at the interface. In the case of TiN/GQD, the work functions of TiN and GQDs are 4.5 eV [59,60] and ≈5.2 eV [58], respectively, which means that at the interface between TiN and GQDs, free electrons will move from TiN to GQDs until the Fermi levels match. As a result of TiOC covalent bonding, TiN becomes positively charged, whereas GQDs become negatively charged, leading to the formation of a heterojunction. Furthermore, GQDs have a small lateral dimension; therefore, they cannot fully counteract the electric field that has been built up. Based on the information presented in Fig. 6c, the findings of this study indicate the accumulation of unbound electrons in proximity to the surface of the GQDs. Consequently, TiN/GQD nanocomposites have promising charge-transfer kinetics and accelerated diffusion of ions and electrons.

Furthermore, the presence of zigzag edges on the GQDs contributes to the concentration of free electrons along these edges, originating from the TiN. This synergistic outcome enhances the specific capacitance by causing unattached electrons to aggregate at the edges of the GQDs, generating an additional electrostatic attraction with sodium ions. As a result, the electrolyte cations are utilized more effectively. One of the crucial aspects of utilizing GQDs in heterostructure design is the size of GQDs. It should be emphasized that GQDs with a larger size have a smaller energy bandgap, and their use in the preparation of heterostructures leads to the Fermi level of GQDs approaching the Fermi level of TiN (compared to the smaller GQDs, whose Fermi level has a wider distance from the Fermi level of TiN). As a result, fewer electrons were transferred from the TiN nanotubes to the GQDs because of the closer Fermi level. Therefore, the utilization ratio of the electrolyte cations decreases, and consequently, the capacitance decreases. Additionally, enlarging the GQDs reduces surface defects such as edge zigzags. In this way, the areal capacitance decreases because surface zigzags are suitable locations for accumulating cations and storing energy based on the electrical double-layer mechanism. In addition, using very small GQDs can widen the energy bandgap, resulting in decreased electrical conductivity. Hence, it could be valuable to investigate the impact of GQDs size on the electrochemical characteristics of heterostructures, aiming to finely adjust their dimensions for optimal performance in future research undertakings.

TiN/GQD nanocomposite electrodes have outstanding merits that lead to their extraordinary electrochemical performance, some of which are mentioned here. First, the lack of a binder improves electrical conductivity between the active components and the current collector. Second, nanotubes have porous morphologies that facilitate electrolyte ion transfer. Third, loading GQDs onto TiN nanotubes increases the surface area of the electrode. The kinetics of the energy storage reactions can be accelerated because the electrode has surface defects and edge sites. The heterostructure preparation resulted in an increase in the capacitance and a 2 V potential window. Because of the innovative design of the TiN/GQD nanocomposite used as the anode and cathode in a symmetrical capacitor, it is anticipated that the performance of these composites will serve as a milestone for the research of metal nitride heterostructures in the application of supercapacitors. These findings show that symmetric capacitors with TiN base electrodes perform exceptionally well, and provide a foundation for future electrode materials for supercapacitors.

To investigate the final performance of the symmetric supercapacitor prepared, a setup was built, as shown in Fig. 10 . In this setup, a homemade boost converter (0.5 V input with about 4 V output) is realized using a toroid-tapped solenoid, resistor, and a transistor coupled with a white LED. The minimum voltage for an LED to turn on was about 3.2 V at a current of less than 5 mA. This boost converter is based on the well-known “Joules Thief” flyback oscillator circuit [61].

This circuit oscillates at about 350 kHz based on the electrical characteristics of the 2N2222 transistor used here. The supercapacitor was initially charged fully up to 1.5 V for about 20 s. Then, the charged supercapacitor was connected to the converter as shown in Fig. 10. The supercapacitor was able to turn on the LED for about 15 s. Initially at its full brightness, then gradually decreasing brightness to down over a period of 20 s. This shows that the fabricated supercapacitor can be used as effective charge storage and delivery device.
Shortly, a nanocomposite electrode of TiN/GQD was synthesized through an uncomplicated and inexpensive approach.

A novel heterostructure was introduced, which not only expanded the attainable operational potential range from −0.5 to 1.5 V, but also elevated the areal capacitance to 190 mF cm-2, thereby highlighting its exceptional capabilities. It was also confirmed that using only low amounts of GQDs can improve the electrochemical behavior of the nanocomposite, and more amounts will lead to adverse effects and loss of performance. Moreover, the heterostructure exhibited strong rate capability, retaining 91.5 % of its capacitance after 6000 cycles. This allowed for the creation of a symmetric supercapacitor with exceptional electrochemical characteristics, including a specific capacitance of 14.7 F g−1 at a current density of 0.2 mA cm−2 and energy density of 8.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 77 W kg−1.
